content.text:
*"If any of you lacks wisdom, you should ask God, who 
gives generously to all without finding fault."*{James 1:5}{Probability is nothing but common sense reduced to calculation.}{Pierre-Simon Laplace}

New information changes what we believe. **Conditional probability** 
updates probabilities in light of evidence.

content.title: Conditional Probability
content.content:
The **conditional probability** of 
$E$ given $F$, denoted $P(E | F)$, is:
\[ P(E | F) = \frac{P(E \cap F)}{P(F)} \quad \text{(provided } P(F) > 0\text{)} \]

content.text:
$P(E | F)$ is the probability of $E$ within the reduced sample space 
where $F$ has occurred.

content.title: Dice
content.problem:
Two dice are rolled. Given the sum is at least 9, what is the probability 
both dice show 5 or higher?

content.title: Independence
content.content:
Events $E$ and $F$ are **independent** if:
\[ P(E \cap F) = P(E) \cdot P(F) \]
Equivalently, $P(E | F) = P(E)$—knowing $F$ doesn't change the 
probability of $E$.

content.title: Independence
content.problem:
A card is drawn, noted, and replaced. Another card is drawn. Are the 
draws independent?

Yes: each draw has the same probabilities regardless of the other.

$P(\text{both hearts}) = (1/4)(1/4) = 1/16$.

content.title: Independence vs. Disjoint
content.content:
Independent events can occur together; disjoint events cannot. If $E$ 
and $F$ are disjoint with positive probability, they are *not* 
independent: $P(E \cap F) = 0 \neq P(E) \cdot P(F)$.

content.text:
Bayes' theorem "inverts" conditional probabilities: from $P(F | E)$ we 
can compute $P(E | F)$.

content.title: Medical testing
content.problem:
A disease affects 1\% of the population. A test is 95\% accurate (detects 
the disease if present; also 95\% specific—negative if no disease). 
If you test positive, what is the probability you have the disease?
content.solution:
Let $D$ = has disease, $+$ = positive test.

$P(D) = 0.01$, $P(+ | D) = 0.95$, $P(+ | \bar{D}) = 0.05$.

$P(+) = P(+ | D)P(D) + P(+ | \bar{D})P(\bar{D}) = 0.95(0.01) + 0.05(0.99) = 0.059$.

$P(D | +) = \frac{0.95 \cdot 0.01}{0.059} \approx 0.161$.

Even with a positive test, there's only a 16\% chance of having the disease!

content.title: Thomas Bayes (1701--1761)
content.content:
Bayes was an English Presbyterian minister and mathematician. His theorem, 
published posthumously, provides the foundation for Bayesian statistics 
and inference. Bayes saw no conflict between his faith and his 
mathematics; both involved reasoning carefully from evidence to conclusions.

marginNotes.text:
In a lawsuit the first to speak seems 
right, until someone comes forward and cross-examines.
